title: Systemd strange behavior
body:
### Issue Description

I use systemd for run batch job by timers (about ~200 per host)
Sometimes I have strange behavior. When I type `systemctl start <service>` or service trying to start, it fails. In logs I see that systemd try to start process but `Main process exited, code=killed, status=9/KILL` and it fails. In this moment I see service container in `podman ps` output in `Exited` status. When I delete this container, all works well.

Do you have any idea, what can be a reason, how diagnose and reproduce it?

```
# podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.1
API Version:  4.3.1
Go Version:   go1.19.3
Git Commit:   814b7b003cc630bf6ab188274706c383f9fb9915-dirty
Built:        Wed Nov 30 04:53:39 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```
```
# apt policy podman
podman:
  Installed: 100:4.3.1-1
  Candidate: 100:4.3.1-1
  Version table:
 *** 100:4.3.1-1 100
        100 /var/lib/dpkg/status
     100:3.4.2-5 500
        500 http://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:/kubic:/libcontainers:/stable/xUbuntu_20.04  Packages

```


### Steps to reproduce the issue

I have no idea why this happened and how to reproduce it :disappointed: , this behavior is very flasky.

### Describe the results you received

All `Exited` containers stuck systemd service

### Describe the results you expected

I expect that when service start, it will ignore all `Exited` containers and works well.

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

My systemd unit look like
```
[Unit]
Description=desc
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
TimeoutStopSec=15
TimeoutStartSec=3600
Type=oneshot
SuccessExitStatus=0 12

ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run \
  --pid=host \
  --replace \
  --cgroups=no-conmon \
  -u www-data \
  --name name \
  --entrypoint='/bin/bash' \
  <image>:<tag> \
  -c "php bin/console <command>"

ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop --ignore --time 10 name
ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/podman rm -f --ignore name
KillMode=process

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

### Additional information

It looks similar with
- https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/6549
- https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/11304
but all advices doesn't solve my issue :crying_cat_face:

comments.body: No idea, but have you looked into using quadlet for services running under systemd?
First time hear about it, looks interesting, thank you.

This is a known issue when running `podman --cgroups=no-conmon $otherOpts` from systemd service.
The `no-conmon` option means, that MAINPID is the PID of `conmon` process, and it runs in its own cgroup, whereas the actual entrypoint of a container is started in a separate cgroup.

Try running the following command to prove this:
```bash
journalctl -a | grep '(conmon) with signal SIGKILL'
# example.service: Killing process 2450 (conmon) with signal SIGKILL.
```

Without a custom `ExecStop` command, systemd sends a SIGTERM signal to $MAINPID (in this case, to `conmon`).
In turn, `conmon` sends a SIGTERM to the PID of the container entrypoint.
For some reason, entrypoint does not stop within 90 seconds (see `systemctl show example.service | grep TimeoutStopUSec`). Therefore, systemd sends SIGKILL to all processes in the cgroup of $MAINPID, which contains only the `conmon` process.

As a result, the container entrypoint keep on running, but it is adopted by PID1, because its parent `conmon` process was killed by systemd.
Also, the podman database shows the `Stopping` container state, because [it is a job of `conmon` to change the state from `Stopping` to `Stopped`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/v4.6.1/libpod/container_internal.go#L1290).

Probably, #19629 has the same issue of `conmon` being killed by systemd upon stop.

One workaround would be to extend the systemd unit with a custom `ExecStop` command, which will 
1) send SIGTERM to $MAINPID (i.e. to conmon);
2) obtain the actual container cgroup by running `cgroup=$(podman inspect $container_name | jq -r '.[].State.CgroupPath')`;
3) kill all processes in the container cgroup by running `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/$cgroup/cgroup.procs | xargs kill ; sleep 5; cat /sys/fs/cgroup/$cgroup/cgroup.procs | xargs kill --signal SIGKILL`

This should clean up all process not exiting after receiving a SIGTERM from the conmon. In turn, this will let `conmon` exit in 90 seconds and not to be killed by systemd.

@aneustroev, if you'd write such custom ExecStop script and it helps, let us know!

quote from https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd.service.html#TimeoutStopSec=
```
TimeoutStopSec=
This option serves two purposes. 
First, it configures the time to wait for each ExecStop= command. 
If any of them times out, 
subsequent ExecStop= commands are skipped and the service will be terminated by SIGTERM. 
If no ExecStop= commands are specified, the service gets the SIGTERM immediately. 
This default behavior can be changed by the TimeoutStopFailureMode= option. 
Second, it configures the time to wait for the service itself to stop. 
If it doesn't terminate in the specified time, it will be forcibly terminated by SIGKILL 
(https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd.kill.html#). 
Takes a unit-less value in seconds, or a time span value such as "5min 20s". 
Pass "infinity" to disable the timeout logic. 
Defaults to DefaultTimeoutStopSec= from the manager configuration file 
(https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd-system.conf.html#).

If a service of Type=notify/Type=notify-reload sends "EXTEND_TIMEOUT_USEC=…", 
this may cause the stop time to be extended beyond TimeoutStopSec=. 
The first receipt of this message must occur before TimeoutStopSec= is exceeded, 
and once the stop time has extended beyond TimeoutStopSec=, 
the service manager will allow the service to continue to stop, 
provided the service repeats "EXTEND_TIMEOUT_USEC=…" within the interval specified, 
or terminates itself (https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/sd_notify.html#).
```
Your solution will be worked only if `TimeoutStopSec=infinity`. I suggest you to use it, when you render systemd unit files. It disable killing by systemd and full relay on `ExecStop`

BTW I can't use podman because it have very bad performance when I try run over 200-300 containers per minute. Don't ask me why I want to do this :smile: 

Thank you for help!
Where are the perfomance issues? Would running podman as a service and then just hammering at it with podman-python work better?
I used podman as service and run containers via hashicorp Nomad.
